name: Hierapolis
description: Hierapolis, meaning 'Holy City' in Greek, holds a significant place in Turkish mythology and Anatolian history, blending ancient pagan beliefs with later Islamic influences. While not directly featured in core Turkic creation myths, its association with thermal springs and natural phenomena led to its integration into local Anatolian Turkic folklore. The Pamukkale travertine terraces, a natural wonder adjacent to Hierapolis, were often interpreted through a lens of divine intervention and miraculous creation. Local legends attributed healing properties of the thermal waters to the blessings of ancient deities, possibly remnants of Phrygian and Roman religious practices absorbed into Turkish folk traditions. The city's ruins, including temples and necropolises, served as a tangible link to a mystical past, inspiring stories of powerful spirits and forgotten gods residing within its ancient stones. The presence of the Plutonium, a cave emitting poisonous gases, further fueled the city's association with the underworld and supernatural forces. Over time, Hierapolis transitioned from a pagan center to a Byzantine stronghold and eventually came under Turkish rule. This layering of cultures and beliefs contributed to a rich tapestry of folklore, where ancient gods coexisted with Islamic saints and mythical creatures, making Hierapolis a potent symbol of Anatolia's syncretic religious heritage. The site's association with healing and the afterlife persists in modern Turkish culture, drawing visitors seeking both physical and spiritual rejuvenation. The visual spectacle of Pamukkale reinforces the sense of a sacred and otherworldly landscape, ensuring Hierapolis remains a powerful site of myth and legend.
subtitle: Holy City of Healing Waters

keyMyths.title: Healing Waters of Pamukkale
keyMyths.description: Legend says that the thermal waters of Pamukkale were blessed by ancient gods, granting them miraculous healing properties. People travel from afar to bathe in these waters, seeking relief from various ailments.

keyMyths.title: The Plutonium's Deadly Breath
keyMyths.description: The Plutonium, a cave in Hierapolis, was believed to be a gateway to the underworld, emitting poisonous gases that could kill anyone who entered. It was said to be guarded by spirits of the dead and ruled by underworld deities.

sources.author: Strabo
sources.description: Ancient Greek geographer's description of Hierapolis and its Plutonium.
sources.text: Ancient Greek geographer's description of Hierapolis and its Plutonium.
